Long ago, before the sky had color, the Wind and the Mountain lived in silence. The Mountain was proud and unmoving, hoarding all the stones of the earth. The Wind was restless and invisible, possessing nothing but its own breath.

One day, the Wind whispered to the Mountain, "Let me carry your smallest stones to the edge of the world. In return, I will sing you songs of what lies beyond the horizon." The Mountain, bored of its eternal stillness, agreed but offered only a handful of dust.

The Wind carried the dust high, swirling it until it caught the light of the sun. Suddenly, the sky turned a brilliant blue, and the dust became clouds that painted shadows on the land. Delighted, the Mountain gave more—pebbles this time—which the Wind polished into stars. Finally, the Mountain gave its jagged peaks, which the Wind carved into valleys where rivers could flow.

They realized that by giving away pieces of themselves, they had created a world far more beautiful than the silence they once knew. To this day, the Wind still howls around the peaks, singing of the world they built together, and the Mountain stands tall, listening.

The River was a restless wanderer, crashing against rocks in a desperate bid to reach the sea, eroding everything in its violent path. They discovered that strength is not just standing tall, nor just moving fast. Their alliance created an ecosystem where the fish could swim and the eagle could nest. Now, the waterfall is their joyful laughter, the place where the stillness of stone and the rush of water celebrate their vital pact.
